items.q: What is the function of the bridge in a pop song?
items.correct: To provide contrast before the final chorus, often by changing melody, key, or texture.
items.misconception: It is the same as the chorus.
items.random_wrong: It is the very first section.
items.why: Students confuse bridge with chorus because both are 'big' sections. The bridge appears once (usually after the second chorus) to provide CONTRAST and renew interest before returning.

items.correct: A cadence ending on chord V, creating a sense of incompleteness.
items.misconception: A cadence that sounds wrong or is performed badly.
items.random_wrong: A cadence using only major chords.
items.why: Students think 'imperfect' means flawed. It's a technical term for an unfinished-sounding cadence - any chord moving to V leaves the music suspended, awaiting resolution.

items.q: What is a balanced antecedent-consequent phrase?
items.correct: Two phrases of equal length where the first sounds open (question) and the second resolves (answer).
items.misconception: Any two phrases of similar style.
items.random_wrong: A long melody with no breaks.
items.why: Students miss the FUNCTIONAL relationship. The antecedent typically ends on an imperfect cadence (open); the consequent answers with a perfect cadence (closed). Length matches; cadences contrast.
It's I-IV-V. “I-ii-iii” is the classic near-miss here; knowing why it's wrong is worth as much as knowing the right answer.

items.q: Reusing audio in a new track is called?
items.why: Correct answer: Sampling. “Mixing” sounds right because it borrows the language of the topic — but it fails this question. Re-read the notes on why.

items.q: How many bars in a standard blues chorus?
items.why: The real figure is 12 bars. The wrong options cluster deliberately close — anchor the exact number to one concrete fact so it can't drift.
